verses.flow: The word of YHWH that came to Micah the Morashtite in the days of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah, which he saw concerning Samaria and Jerusalem.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Micah the Morashtite was from Moresheth-gath (cf. v.14), a town in the Shephelah region of Judah, southwest of Jerusalem. His ministry spanned the reigns of Jotham (750-735 BC), Ahaz (735-715 BC), and Hezekiah (715-686 BC), making him a contemporary of Isaiah and Hosea. His prophetic vision (chazah, H2372) encompasses both the northern capital Samaria and the southern capital Jerusalem, framing a message of universal judgment.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal jussive 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Hear, O peoples, all of you; attend, O earth, and all that fills it; and let my Lord YHWH be a witness against you, my Lord from his holy temple.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The call "Hear, O peoples" (shim'u ammim, H8085/H5971) invokes a cosmic courtroom scene in which YHWH serves as witness (ed, H5707) against the nations. The phrase "my Lord YHWH" uses Adonai (H136) alongside the Tetragrammaton. The "holy temple" (hekal qodsho) may refer simultaneously to the earthly Jerusalem sanctuary and the heavenly divine council chamber, paralleling Psalm 82:1 and Isaiah 6:1. (Ps 82:1, Isa 6:1)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: For behold, YHWH is coming out from his place, and he will come down and tread upon the high places (bamot) of the earth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's "coming out from his place" (yatza mimqomo, H3318/H4725) is a theophanic descent formula, denoting divine abandonment of his sanctuary and the onset of judgment. The trampling of the "high places" (bamot, H1116) is doubly significant: bamot were literal Canaanite cultic sites adopted by Israel, and their destruction signals YHWH's reclamation of creation's high places from false worship. TYPOLOGY: This theophanic imagery anticipates the final descent of YHWH in Revelation 19. (Deut 32:13, Amos 4:13, Rev 19:11-15)

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

words.g: the a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: And the mountains shall melt beneath him, and the valleys shall be split open, like wax before the fire, like waters poured down a steep place.

All this is for the transgression (pesha) of Jacob and for the sins of the house of Israel. What is the transgression of Jacob? Is it not Samaria? And what are the high places of Judah? Are they not Jerusalem?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Transgression" (pesha, H6588) carries the sense of deliberate rebellion or breach of covenant, stronger than mere inadvertent sin (chata, H2398). By identifying Samaria and Jerusalem as the embodiment of Israel's and Judah's sin respectively, Micah indicts the capitals and their sanctioned worship systems — the political and religious elite — rather than the rural poor. This rhetorical structure is characteristic of Micah's social critique. (Amos 3:9-10, Mic 3:1-4)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing

words.g: to/for a gorge (from its lofty sides

verses.flow: Therefore I will make Samaria a heap of ruins in the open country, a place for planting vineyards; and I will pour down her stones into the valley, and uncover her foundations.

All her carved images shall be beaten to pieces, all her wages of prostitution shall be burned with fire, and all her idols I will lay waste; for from the wages of a prostitute she gathered them, and to the wages of a prostitute they shall return.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "wages of prostitution" (etnan zonah, H868/H2181) likely refers to the wealth accumulated through syncretistic temple prostitution and idolatrous trade at Samaria's cult sites. The irony of the verse is deliberate: wealth gained for idol-worship will be recycled as spoils for the conquering nation's own idols. Micah here echoes Hosea's metaphor of Israel as an adulterous wife. (Hos 2:12, Deut 23:18)

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil cohortative 1st common sing

words.g: as/like a marine or land monster

verses.flow: For this I will lament and wail; I will go stripped and naked; I will make lamentation like the jackals, and mourning like the daughters of the ostrich.
For her wound is incurable (anush); for it has come to Judah, it has reached to the gate of my people, to Jerusalem.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Incurable" (anush, H605) is the same root used in Jeremiah 17:9 for the deceitful heart ("desperately sick"). The wound (makah, H4347) that began in Samaria has now metastasized to reach Judah's own gate. This verse serves as the theological hinge of the chapter: the judgment is not contained but spreads southward, reaching Jerusalem itself. (Jer 17:9, Isa 1:5-6)

verses.flow: Tell it not in Gath; weep not at all; in Beth-le-aphrah roll yourselves in the dust.
verses.note: HEBREW: Verse 10 opens with an allusion to David's lament: "Tell it not in Gath" (al-tagidu b'Gath) echoes 2 Samuel 1:20, invoking the memory of national catastrophe. Micah then employs an extended series of wordplays (paronomasia) through verses 10-15, matching the town names with their fates — a literary device with roots in prophetic lament poetry. "Beth-le-aphrah" means "house of dust," making the command to "roll in dust" a bitter pun on the city's own name. (2 Sam 1:20)

verses.flow: Pass on your way, O inhabitant of Shaphir, in nakedness and shame; the inhabitant of Zaanan does not come out; the lamentation of Beth-ezel shall take away from you its standing place.

words.g: to/for + in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: For the inhabitant of Maroth writhes in pain waiting for good, because disaster has come down from YHWH to the gate of Jerusalem.
Harness the steeds to the chariots, O inhabitant of Lachish — she was the beginning of sin to the daughter of Zion — for in you were found the transgressions of Israel.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Lachish was the second most important city in Judah, a major military and administrative center in the Shephelah. Archaeological excavations have confirmed its destruction by Sennacherib in 701 BC, corroborating the prophetic context. Micah's accusation that Lachish was the "beginning of sin" (reshit chat'at, H7225/H2403) to Zion may indicate that northern Israelite idolatrous practices entered Judah via Lachish's trade routes. (2 Kgs 18:14, Isa 36:2)

verses.flow: Therefore you shall give parting gifts to Moresheth-gath; the houses of Achzib shall be a deceitful thing to the kings of Israel.
I will again bring a conqueror to you, O inhabitant of Mareshah; the glory of Israel shall come to Adullam.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "The glory of Israel shall come to Adullam" is a cryptic but powerful statement. Adullam was the cave where David and his mighty men — the rejected and dispossessed — gathered (1 Sam 22:1). Micah may be anticipating the humiliation of the Judean elite, driven like David's outcasts to caves. On a deeper typological level, this foreshadows the Son of David who, rejected, gathers the broken and dispossessed around himself before his ultimate vindication. (1 Sam 22:1-2, Matt 5:3-5)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem sing

verses.flow: Make yourselves bald and cut off your hair, for the children of your delight; make your baldness as wide as the eagle, for they shall go from you into exile.
verses.note: HEBREW: Shaving the head bald (qorcha, H7144) and cutting the hair were ancient Near Eastern signs of extreme mourning, normally prohibited for Israelites in certain contexts (Deut 14:1) but invoked here as an intensified lament. The comparison to the "eagle" (nesher, H5404) — likely the griffon vulture, whose bald head was a recognized image — heightens the grimness of the exile imagery. "Children of your delight" (bene ta'anugayikh) are the beloved children carried into Assyrian captivity. (Deut 14:1, Amos 8:10, 2 Kgs 17:6)

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Woe to those who devise iniquity and work evil upon their beds! In the light of the morning they do it, because it is in the power of their hand.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Devise iniquity" (חָשַׁב אָוֶן, hashav aven; Strong's H2803, H205) — the verb hashav ("to think, plan, devise") is the same root used of skilled craftsmen and of divine wisdom, here grotesquely inverted. The phrase "upon their beds" emphasizes premeditated sin; the wicked scheme through the night and execute at dawn. This mirrors the covenantal curses of Leviticus 26 falling upon covenant-breakers.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: And they covet fields and seize them, and houses, and take them away; they oppress a man and his house, a man and his inheritance.
Therefore thus says YHWH: Behold, I am devising against this family a calamity, from which you shall not remove your necks, nor shall you walk upright; for it is an evil time.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's response mirrors the crime precisely — the oppressors "devised" (hashav) evil; now YHWH "devises" (hashav) calamity against them. This is the lex talionis operating at a covenantal level. The "family" (mishpachah) indicted may refer to the ruling house or the nation corporately as a covenant household.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: In that day they shall take up a taunt-song against you and wail with a bitter wailing, and say, "We are utterly ruined! He changes the portion of my people; how he removes it from me! To the apostate he allots our fields."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Taunt-song" (מָשָׁל, mashal; Strong's H4912) denotes a proverb, parable, or mocking byword. The Hebrew of this verse is notoriously compressed and difficult; the shift between speakers mid-verse is intentional, dramatizing the chaos of judgment. "He allots our fields to the apostate" (שׁוֹבֵב, shovev) depicts YHWH reassigning the stolen land to those who turned away — a bitter reversal for those who hoarded property.

verses.flow: Therefore you shall have none who casts the line by lot in the assembly of YHWH.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: "Do not preach" — they preach. "They shall not preach of these things; disgrace shall not overtake us."

words.m: particle + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Should this be said, O house of Jacob? Is the Spirit (ruach) of YHWH impatient? Are these his deeds? Do not my words do good to one who walks uprightly?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The question "Is the Spirit (ruach) of YHWH impatient?" challenges the false comfort of those who presume YHWH will not act in judgment. The Hebrew ruach (Strong's H7307) — spirit, wind, breath — is the animating presence and power of YHWH. The rhetorical questions expose a theology of presumption: the people have domesticated YHWH into a deity who only blesses. Compare Isaiah 63:10 where Israel "grieved his Holy Spirit."

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: But of late my people have risen up as an enemy; you strip off the rich robe from those who pass by trustingly, those who are averse to war.

words.g: to drive out from a possession
words.m: verb piel imperfect 2nd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: The women of my people you drive out from their delightful houses; from their young children you take away my splendor forever.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The eviction of women from their homes reflects the brutal social consequences of debt foreclosure and land-seizure in 8th-century Judah. Deuteronomy 14–15 and Leviticus 25 established jubilee and debt-release laws to prevent exactly this accumulation of property among elites. Archaeological evidence from the period confirms a sharp stratification of wealth in Iron Age IIB Judah.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Arise and go, for this is no place of rest; because of uncleanness that destroys with a grievous destruction.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: If a man walking in wind and falsehood should lie, saying, "I will preach to you of wine and strong drink," he would be the preacher for this people!

words.m: verb qal imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

words.g: as/like a collective name for a flock
from ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: I will surely gather you, O Jacob, all of you; I will surely collect the remnant of Israel; I will set them together like sheep of Bozrah, like a flock in the midst of their pasture, they shall be loud with men.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This sudden oracle of restoration (vv. 12–13) is unexpected after sustained judgment, modeling the pattern of Micah's entire book: doom followed by hope. The "remnant" (שְׁאֵרִית, she'erit; Strong's H7611) is a key Micah theme (cf. Mic 4:7; 5:7–8; 7:18). The gathering of scattered Israel anticipates the ingathering of the nations under Messiah (cf. John 10:16; Eph 2:11–22). "Bozrah" may evoke the imagery of a royal sheepfold and later appears in eschatological contexts (Isa 63:1).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: The one who breaks open will go up before them; they will break through and pass the gate, going out by it. Their king will pass on before them, YHWH at their head.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "The one who breaks open" (הַפֹּרֵץ, ha-poretz; Strong's H6555) is a title — the Breaker — whose identity is debated but ultimately finds its fullest meaning in the Messiah who bursts the gates of death and leads his people out (cf. John 10:3–4; Rev 19:11–16). The sequence — Breaker leads, king goes before, YHWH at the head — is a divine warrior procession. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The image of YHWH "at their head" (בְּרֹאשָׁם) as divine warrior-king heading a triumphant procession echoes ancient Near Eastern conquest theology where the national deity marches at the vanguard of his people's armies, decisively reclaiming territory from rival powers.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

words.g: and a magistrate or other leader

verses.flow: And I said: Hear now, O heads of Jacob and rulers of the house of Israel — is it not for you to know justice (mishpat)?
verses.note: HEBREW: The chapter opens with the particle "wa'omar" (and I said), placing Micah himself as the speaker — a rare first-person intrusion that asserts prophetic authority. "Mishpat" (H4941, justice/judgment) is the governing concern of the entire oracle; it denotes not merely fairness but the administration of covenantal order as YHWH defines it. The address to "heads" (ro'shim) and "rulers" (qetsinim) indicts the civil leadership specifically, not the general populace.

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: You who hate the good and love the evil, who tear the skin from off them and their flesh from off their bones;

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: who also eat the flesh of my people, and flay their skin from off them, and break their bones, and chop them in pieces as flesh in a pot, and as meat within a cauldron.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The cannibal-butcher imagery draws on an ancient Near Eastern curse-language for oppression. In its deepest typological register, the rulers who devour the flesh of God's people stand in stark contrast to the Good Shepherd of Ezekiel 34 and John 10 — a contrast Micah himself prepares for in chapter 5 with the ruler from Bethlehem. The brutality described here anticipates the NT condemnation of shepherds who exploit the flock (John 10:12-13).

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then they will cry out to YHWH, but he will not answer them; and he will hide his face from them at that time, according as they have dealt wickedly in their doings.

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man
the to strike with a sting

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH concerning the prophets who lead my people astray, who bite with their teeth and cry "Shalom (shalom)!" — but whoever puts nothing into their mouths, against him they consecrate war:
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shalom" (H7965) here is spoken falsely — a biting irony, as the word that denotes wholeness and covenantal well-being is weaponized by false prophets for personal gain. "Consecrate war" (qidshu milhamah) is a technical cultic term for declaring holy war, here grotesquely turned against those who do not pay the prophet's fee. THEOLOGICAL: This verse exposes the commodification of prophecy — the sin of preaching what congregants pay to hear rather than what YHWH commands.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Therefore, night shall come over you, without vision; and darkness shall come upon you, without divination. The sun shall go down upon the prophets, and the day shall be black over them.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: The seers shall be ashamed, and the diviners confounded; and they shall all cover their lips, for there is no answer from God.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: In ancient Israel and the broader ANE, prophets and diviners held public roles as mediators of divine speech. "Covering the lips" (atu al-saphim) was a gesture of mourning and public shame (cf. Lev 13:45; Ezek 24:17). The silence of false prophets at the moment of crisis contrasts directly with Micah's boldness in verse 8, structuring the passage as an antithesis between counterfeit and authentic prophetic ministry.

words.g: and however or on the contrary
to fill or be full of

verses.flow: But as for me, I am filled with power — with the Spirit (ruach) of YHWH — and with justice (mishpat) and might, to declare to Jacob his transgression and to Israel his sin.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 8 is the theological center of the chapter — a stunning contrast to the venal prophets of verses 5-7. Micah attributes his prophetic power not to skill or status but to the "ruach" (H7307, Spirit/wind/breath) of YHWH. This is one of the clearest OT affirmations of Spirit-empowered prophetic ministry, anticipating the NT gift of the Spirit poured out for proclamation (Acts 2:17-18). The triad of "power, justice, and might" defines authentic prophetic character.

words.g: and a magistrate or other leader

verses.flow: Hear now this, O heads of the house of Jacob and rulers of the house of Israel, who abhor justice (mishpat) and make crooked all that is straight,

words.g: in/with blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: who build Zion with blood and Jerusalem with iniquity.

words.g: and a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: Her heads judge for a bribe, and her priests teach for a price, and her prophets divine for silver; yet they lean upon YHWH and say, "Is not YHWH in our midst? Calamity shall not come upon us."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 11 identifies the deepest corruption: these leaders invoke YHWH's name and presence as a talisman against judgment while simultaneously violating every covenantal obligation. This is a form of presumption theology — reducing YHWH to a protective deity whose presence guarantees national security regardless of ethical conduct. Jesus confronts the same logic in Matthew 7:22-23. HISTORICAL: The hiring of priests and prophets for payment reflects the patronage economy of ancient Israelite cult institutions.

words.g: and a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: Therefore, on account of you, Zion shall be plowed as a field, and Jerusalem shall become heaps of ruins, and the mountain of the house shall become high places of a forest.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Verse 12 is quoted verbatim by the elders in Jeremiah 26:18 as legal precedent for prophetic free speech — the only OT instance where a prophet is explicitly cited by name to justify another prophet's oracle against Jerusalem. This establishes Micah 3:12 as foundational for the theology of prophetic accountability. The fulfilled threat of Jerusalem's destruction (587 BC) and the temple mount becoming overgrown validates Micah's words. TYPOLOGY: The plowing of Zion anticipates both the Roman destruction of Jerusalem (70 AD) and the eschatological renewal of Zion in Micah 4:1-2.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: a mountain or range of hills
the a mountain or range of hills

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: And it shall come to pass in the latter days, that the mountain of the house of YHWH shall be established as the head of the mountains, and it shall be lifted up above the hills; and peoples shall flow to it.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The phrase "latter days" (Hebrew: acharit hayamim, H319) is a technical eschatological marker in the prophets, pointing to a future age of Messianic fulfillment. This verse is nearly identical to Isaiah 2:2, suggesting a shared oracle or liturgical tradition. The exaltation of YHWH's mountain over all others is a direct counter to the divine council cosmology of the ANE, where gods were associated with cosmic mountains (cf. Ps 48:2; Isa 14:13). The NT understands the "mountain of YHWH's house" as realized in the Messianic community gathered to the risen Christ (Heb 12:22).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal cohortative 1st common plur

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal cohortative 1st common plur

verses.flow: And many nations shall come and say, "Come, let us go up to the mountain of YHWH, and to the house of the God of Jacob, that he may teach us from his ways and that we may walk in his paths." For from Zion shall go forth instruction (torah), and the word of YHWH from Jerusalem.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Instruction" here is torah (H8451), not merely "law" in the legal sense, but divine teaching and direction proceeding from God's own character. Its going forth from Zion reverses the Babel-pattern of human striving and replaces it with centripetal divine initiative — the nations come to YHWH rather than building their own tower. THEOLOGICAL: The universality of this verse is striking; the same torah given to Israel becomes the pathway for all nations. This anticipates the Great Commission and the Pentecost ingathering (Acts 2).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: and to bruise or violently strike
words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential perfect 3rd common plur
verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: And he shall judge between many peoples, and shall arbitrate for strong nations afar off; and they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks; nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The beating of swords into plowshares is the definitive image of Messianic shalom (peace/wholeness). Joel 3:10 reverses this image for the day of battle, showing these oracles are eschatologically framed. The cessation of war "learning" implies a deep anthropological transformation — war as a practice must be unlearned, pointing to the renewal of the human heart. NT fulfillment: Christ is described as our peace (shalom) who has broken down the dividing wall (Eph 2:14-17).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: But they shall sit, each man under his vine and under his fig tree, and none shall make them afraid; for the mouth of YHWH of hosts has spoken.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Each man under his vine and under his fig tree" (cf. 1 Kgs 4:25; Zech 3:10) is a well-attested image of royal prosperity and covenant blessing in ancient Israel and the ANE. It evokes Solomon's reign as a type of the coming Messianic kingdom's security and abundance. The phrase "none shall make them afraid" echoes the covenant blessing of Leviticus 26:6, signaling full covenantal restoration.

verses.flow: For all the peoples walk, each in the name of its god, but we will walk in the name of YHWH our God forever and ever.

words.m: conjunction + particle + verb niphal participle active

verses.flow: In that day, declares YHWH, I will assemble the lame, and gather the outcast, even her whom I have afflicted;

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

words.g: and + the to remove or be remote
words.m: conjunction + particle + verb niphal participle passive
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: in/with a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: and I will make the lame a remnant, and her who was cast off a strong nation; and YHWH will reign over them in Mount Zion from this time forth and forevermore.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The assertion that "YHWH will reign over them in Mount Zion from this time forth and forevermore" is a bold declaration of YHWH's direct and everlasting kingship. This stands against both the immediate political crisis and the cosmic backdrop of rival divine claims. The remnant (she'erit, H7611) theology is central to Micah: judgment does not mean annihilation but a purified, restored community (cf. Mic 5:7-8; Rom 9:27).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: And you, O tower of the flock (migdal-eder), O hill of the daughter of Zion — to you it shall come, the former dominion shall come, the kingdom of the daughter of Jerusalem.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Migdal-eder" (H4029, tower of the flock) appears also in Genesis 35:21 near Bethlehem, where Jacob camped after Rachel's death. Early Jewish tradition (Targum Pseudo-Jonathan on Gen 35:21) connects this location with the future revelation of the Messiah-King. TYPOLOGY: The proximity of this Bethlehem-adjacent term to the Messianic birth oracle of Micah 5:2 is theologically significant and was likely understood messianically by Second Temple readers.

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Now why do you cry aloud? Is there no king in you? Has your counselor perished, that pain has seized you like a woman in labor?

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem sing

words.g: and to reside or permanently stay
words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd fem sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd fem sing

words.g: to be the next of kin
words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

words.g: from the hollow hand or palm

verses.flow: Writhe and groan, O daughter of Zion, like a woman in labor; for now you shall go out from the city and dwell in the open country; you shall go to Babylon. There you shall be rescued; there YHWH shall redeem you from the hand of your enemies.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The mention of Babylon by name is remarkable and debated. Micah prophesied in the late 8th century BC, when Assyria was the dominant threat — Babylon's rise to supremacy came later. Some scholars see this as genuine predictive prophecy; others posit later editorial activity. THEOLOGICAL: Regardless, the structure of the oracle is clear: captivity is not the end. YHWH promises both the exile (judgment) and the redemption from it, establishing his sovereign control over both movements of history (cf. Jer 29:10-14).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal jussive 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: And now many nations are assembled against you, saying, "Let her be defiled, and let our eyes gaze upon Zion."

words.m: verb piel perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: But they do not know the thoughts of YHWH, and they do not understand his counsel; for he has gathered them as sheaves to the threshing floor.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "thoughts of YHWH" (machshevot YHWH, H4284) contrasts the limited perspective of the nations with YHWH's sovereign hidden counsel. This is a wisdom-warfare motif: the nations assemble thinking themselves conquerors, but they are unwitting instruments in YHWH's harvesting judgment. The image of gathering as sheaves to a threshing floor appears also in divine warfare contexts (Jer 51:33; Rev 14:14-20), where YHWH is the divine harvester.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem sing

words.g: and a claw or split hoof

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 2nd fem sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Arise and thresh, O daughter of Zion, for I will make your horn iron and your hoofs bronze; and you shall beat in pieces many peoples; and you shall devote their gain to YHWH, and their wealth to the Lord of all the earth.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Horn" (qeren, H7161) is a standard Hebrew metaphor for power and strength, often used of kings and nations (Deut 33:17; Ps 75:10; Dan 7-8). "Devote" (hacharam, H2763) is the language of the cherem — sacred destruction devoted entirely to YHWH, as in holy war. THEOLOGICAL: The concluding declaration "Lord of all the earth" (adon kol-ha'arets) is a title used at liminal moments of YHWH's cosmic assertion of sovereignty (Josh 3:11, 13; Zech 4:14; 6:5), affirming that Israel's victory is ultimately a declaration of YHWH's universal reign.

verses.flow: Now you shall muster your troops, O daughter of troops; siege is laid against us; with a rod they strike the judge of Israel upon the cheek.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The opening verse (numbered v. 1 in English; v. 4:14 in Hebrew) addresses Jerusalem as "daughter of troops" under siege — likely the Assyrian assault of Sennacherib (701 BC) or, proleptically, the Babylonian siege. The striking of the judge on the cheek is a gesture of profound humiliation in ANE legal culture, echoing the mockery of Israel's royal leadership. TYPOLOGY: The struck judge anticipates the Servant who is struck and shamed (Isa 50:6), fulfilled in the striking of Jesus before the Sanhedrin (Matt 26:67).

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: But you, O Bethlehem Ephrathah, who are too little to be among the clans ('alphey) of Judah, from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler (moshel) in Israel, whose goings forth are from of old, from the days of eternity ('olam).
verses.note: HEBREW: "'Alphey" (Strong's H504) means "clans" or "thousands," indicating Bethlehem's insignificance in tribal mustering lists. "Moshel" (H4910) denotes a ruler or one who governs with authority. "'Olam" (H5769) carries the sense of antiquity stretching to the horizon of time — "ancient days" or "eternity," pointing to the pre-existence of the coming ruler. TYPOLOGY: This is the most direct messianic prophecy in Micah, cited verbatim in Matt 2:6 and alluded to in John 7:42. The Bethlehem origin combined with eternal "goings forth" (motsa'otav) forms the tension between incarnate humanity and divine pre-existence resolved only in the person of Jesus the Messiah (John 1:1-14; Col 1:17).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: and + in/with to tend a flock
words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: Therefore he shall give them up until the time when she who is in labor has given birth; then the rest of his brothers shall return to the people of Israel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "She who is in labor" likely carries a double reference — to Israel's travail under exile and, typologically, to the birth of the Messiah himself (cf. Isa 7:14; 66:7-8; Rev 12:1-5). The giving up of Israel until the birth signals a redemptive-historical interval between promise and fulfillment. The "rest of his brothers" returning echoes the regathering theme central to the new exodus hope.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common plur

verses.flow: And he shall stand and shepherd his flock in the strength of YHWH, in the majesty of the name of YHWH his God. And they shall dwell secure, for now he shall be great to the ends of the earth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The shepherd-king imagery unites the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7) with the divine warrior who stands to rule. Ruling "in the strength of YHWH" and "in the majesty (ga'on) of the name of YHWH his God" affirms that the Messiah's authority is derived from and subordinate to YHWH, yet he himself bears that name before the nations. TYPOLOGY: Jesus as the Good Shepherd who rules to the ends of the earth fulfills this portrait (John 10:11; Phil 2:9-11).

words.g: and + in/with to tend a flock
words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And this one shall be their shalom (shalom), when the Assyrian comes into our land and treads in our palaces, then we will raise against him seven shepherds and eight princes of men.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shalom" (H7965) here functions as a title or predicate — "this one shall be peace (shalom)" — not merely a state of peace but the Messiah himself as the embodiment and agent of shalom. THEOLOGICAL: The Assyrian represents the archetypal imperial enemy, and the seven shepherds and eight princes is a wisdom formula (cf. Eccl 11:2) signifying complete, overflowing provision of leadership. TYPOLOGY: Christ as "our peace" (Eph 2:14) who breaks down the dividing wall is the ultimate fulfillment of this peace-bringer language.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: They shall shepherd the land of Assyria with the sword, and the land of Nimrod at its entrances; and he shall deliver us from the Assyrian when he comes into our land and when he treads within our border.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: a collective name for a flock

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: and to pluck off or pull to pieces
words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then the remnant (she'erit) of Jacob shall be in the midst of many peoples like dew from YHWH, like showers upon the grass, which delay not for a man nor wait for the children of man.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The remnant's blessing is described as coming "like dew from YHWH" — dew in the ANE was understood as a direct divine gift not subject to human agency or natural mechanisms controlled by Baal (cf. Hos 14:5; Deut 33:28). This passage asserts YHWH's exclusive sovereignty over blessing, undercutting rival divine patrons of fertility. THEOLOGICAL: The remnant's role among the nations is passive and gracious — a channel of divine blessing — contrasting with the active, conquering image of v. 8, revealing the two-sided nature of Israel's missional presence.

verses.flow: And the remnant of Jacob shall be among the nations, in the midst of many peoples, like a lion among the beasts of the forest, like a young lion among the flocks of sheep, who, when it passes through, treads down and tears in pieces, and there is none to deliver.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The lion imagery draws on the Judahite royal tradition (Gen 49:9) where Judah/the Davidic king is the lion among nations. The remnant here takes on royal and militant characteristics, suggesting that the scattered community participates in the Messiah's ultimate triumph over hostile nations. This tension between dew (v. 7) and lion (v. 8) reflects the dual aspects of the coming kingdom: grace and judgment.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Your hand shall be lifted up over your adversaries, and all your enemies shall be cut off.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing

words.g: in/with a city in the widest sense
and to pull down or in pieces
words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And in that day, declares YHWH, I will cut off your horses from among you and will destroy your chariots,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal cohortative 1st common sing

verses.flow: and I will cut off the cities of your land and will throw down all your strongholds,

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: and I will cut off sorceries (keshaphim) from your hand, and you shall have no more tellers of fortunes ('oveney);
verses.note: HEBREW: "Keshaphim" (H3785) refers to sorceries or witchcraft, derived from the root kashaph. "'Oveney" (H6049) denotes those who practice divination or observe omens, a class of practitioners condemned throughout the Torah (Deut 18:10-12). THEOLOGICAL: The purging of occult practices is integral to the coming redemption — the removal of false spiritual intermediaries makes way for direct access to YHWH, fulfilled ultimately in Christ who renders all mediatory magic obsolete (Acts 8:9-24; Rev 21:8).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 1st common sing

words.g: in/with a city in the widest sense

verses.flow: and I will cut off your carved images (pesilim) and your pillars (matstsevot) from among you, and you shall bow down no more to the work of your hands;
verses.note: HEBREW: "Pesilim" (H6456) are carved or graven images, from the root pasal, to carve or hew. "Matstsevot" (H4676) are standing stones or sacred pillars, often associated with Canaanite high-place worship and ancestral cult veneration. HISTORICAL: Archaeological evidence from sites such as Arad and Megiddo confirms the prevalence of standing stones in Israelite worship contexts, reflecting the persistent syncretism Micah condemns.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: and I will uproot your Asherah poles ('asherim) from among you and will destroy your cities.
verses.note: HEBREW: "'Asherim" (H842) are wooden poles or stylized trees representing the Canaanite goddess Asherah, consort of El in the Ugaritic divine council tradition. Their uprooting is a polemic against the rival divine feminine figure who had penetrated even official Yahwistic worship (cf. 2 Kgs 21:7; 23:6). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The systematic removal of Asherah poles, carved images, and pillars signals YHWH's reclamation of sole divine prerogative over Israel, dismantling the structures through which territorial and household deities were invoked (Deut 32:17; Ps 82).

words.g: the a mountain or range of hills

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd fem plur

verses.flow: Hear now what YHWH is saying: Arise, contend before the mountains, and let the hills hear your voice.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Micah 6 opens a covenant lawsuit (rib pattern) in which YHWH summons the mountains and foundations of the earth as witnesses — a motif rooted in Deut 32:1 where heaven and earth are called as witnesses to the Mosaic covenant. The call to "arise and contend" (qum riv) signals a formal legal proceeding in the divine court. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The mountains and "enduring foundations" (mosdei arets) may reflect the cosmic witnesses of the heavenly assembly, consistent with Deut 32 geography of nations. (Deut 32:1; Ps 82:1; Isa 1:2)

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: Hear, O mountains, the controversy (riv) of YHWH, and you enduring foundations of the earth; for YHWH has a controversy with His people, and with Israel He will contend.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word "controversy" (riv, Strong's H7379) denotes a legal dispute or lawsuit. Its repeated use in vv. 1-2 establishes the formal juridical tone of the entire chapter. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH as plaintiff against His own covenant people mirrors the structure of Hittite suzerainty treaties, where the great king could indict a vassal for covenant violation. The "enduring foundations" serve as eternal, impartial witnesses. (Isa 1:2; Deut 32:1; Hos 4:1)

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: O My people, what have I done to you? And in what have I wearied you? Testify against Me.

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: For I brought you up from the land of Egypt, and from the house of slavery I redeemed you; and I sent before you Moses, Aaron, and Miriam.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The mention of Moses, Aaron, and Miriam as a triad of leaders is unique in the OT — only here are all three named together as co-leaders of the Exodus. This reflects ancient tradition of Miriam's prophetic leadership (Exod 15:20). YHWH's recounting of redemptive acts is a standard element of the covenant lawsuit, rehearsing the basis of the relationship. (Exod 15:20; Num 20:1; Ps 77:20)

verses.flow: O My people, remember now what Balak king of Moab counseled, and what Balaam son of Beor answered him — from Shittim to Gilgal — that you may know the righteous acts (tsidqot) of YHWH.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Balaam episode (Num 22-24) and the journey from Shittim (the last encampment in Moab) to Gilgal (the first encampment in Canaan) summarize the entire wilderness-to-conquest transition. HEBREW: "Righteous acts" (tsidqot, Strong's H6666, plural of tsedaqah) refers to YHWH's saving deeds of covenant faithfulness — His acts of vindication on behalf of His people. TYPOLOGY: The crossing from Shittim to Gilgal foreshadows the ultimate crossing from bondage to inheritance, fulfilled in Christ's redemption. (Num 22-24; Josh 4:19; Rom 5:8)

words.g: to/for gods in the ordinary sense

words.m: particle + verb piel imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: With what shall I come before YHWH, and bow myself before God on high? Shall I come before Him with burnt offerings, with calves a year old?

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing
particle + verb qal imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Will YHWH be pleased with thousands of rams, with ten thousands of rivers of oil? Shall I give my firstborn for my transgression, the fruit of my body for the sin of my soul?
He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does YHWH require of you but to do justice (mishpat), and to love kindness (hesed), and to walk humbly with your God?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Micah 6:8 stands as one of the great ethical summaries of the entire OT, distilling the prophetic demand into three interlocking imperatives: doing justice (mishpat, Strong's H4941), loving covenant-loyalty (hesed, Strong's H2617), and humble walking (tsana halak) with God. HEBREW: "Humbly" (tsanea, Strong's H6800) occurs only here in the OT — it implies not meekness alone but a careful, attentive walk of dependence. TYPOLOGY: Jesus embodied all three perfectly (Matt 12:18-20; John 13:1-17; Phil 2:8) and fulfills the prophetic ethic from within. (Deut 10:12; Hos 6:6; Matt 23:23; Phil 2:8)

words.g: to/for + in/with a city in the widest sense

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The voice of YHWH calls to the city — and it is wisdom to fear Your name — hear, O tribe, and who has appointed it!
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "it is wisdom to fear Your name" (tushiyyah yirah shemecha) is textually difficult — "tushiyyah" (Strong's H8454) denotes practical wisdom or sound counsel. Some manuscripts read this as a parenthetical note affirming that fearing YHWH's name is itself wisdom. THEOLOGICAL: The unnamed "tribe" and the one who has "appointed" the coming judgment signal both communal accountability and YHWH's sovereign orchestration of discipline. (Prov 1:7; Isa 28:29)

words.g: and an ephah or measure for grain

verses.flow: Are there yet treasures of wickedness in the house of the wicked, and the short measure (ephah) that is accursed?

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Shall I be pure with wicked scales, and with a bag of deceitful weights?

words.g: to fill or be full of

words.m: conjunction + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: For her rich men are full of violence, and her inhabitants have spoken falsehood, and their tongue is deceitful in their mouth.
Therefore I also have made you sick in striking you, making you desolate because of your sins.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "I also have made you sick in striking you" uses a causative construction emphasizing YHWH's direct agency in covenant curse. This recalls the curse formulas of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28, where illness, military defeat, and agricultural failure are covenantal consequences. HEBREW: The Hebrew lehakotka (to strike you) reflects the verb nakah (Strong's H5221), used of divine judgment blows throughout the prophets. (Lev 26:16; Deut 28:22; Amos 4:9)

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: You shall eat but not be satisfied, and your hunger shall be within you; you shall store up but not save, and what you save I will give to the sword.
You shall sow but not reap; you shall tread olives but not anoint yourself with oil; and you shall tread grapes but not drink wine.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The pattern of "sow but not reap / tread but not use" reflects the classic covenant curse of futility (Deut 28:38-40), where labor yields no fruit. This is the reversal of the creation and Promised Land blessing. TYPOLOGY: The ultimate inversion of these curses comes in Christ, who bore covenant curse so that the redeemed inherit an unending harvest. (Deut 28:38-40; Amos 5:11; Gal 3:13; Rev 22:2)

words.m: conjunction + verb hithpael imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: For the statutes of Omri are kept, and all the works of the house of Ahab; and you have walked in their counsels, that I may make you a desolation, and her inhabitants a hissing — and the reproach of My people you shall bear.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Omri and Ahab represent the apex of northern royal apostasy — Omri founded Samaria and introduced systematic state idolatry, while Ahab expanded it through Baal worship (1 Kgs 16:25-33). That Judah is now accused of following Omri's statutes is a severe indictment, collapsing the distinction between the two kingdoms in terms of covenant unfaithfulness. THEOLOGICAL: "A hissing" (sheruqah, Strong's H8322) denotes the shuddering contempt of horrified passersby — the exact opposite of the honor YHWH intended for His people among the nations. (1 Kgs 16:25-33; Jer 18:16; Deut 28:37)

verses.flow: Woe is me! For I have become as the gatherings of summer fruit, as the gleanings of the grape harvest — there is no cluster to eat, no first-ripe fig that my soul desires.
verses.note: HEBREW: The opening "Allelai li" (woe is me) echoes lament psalms. The imagery of gleaning after harvest (Strong's H5955, 'oleloth) pictures a land morally stripped bare. The "first-ripe fig" (bikkurah, H1063) was a delicacy, signifying what is most desirable — Micah mourns its absence in human society. Compare Jeremiah's similar lament (Jer 5:1).

words.g: in/with ruddy i.e. a human being
to/for blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: The faithful (hasid) man has perished from the earth, and there is none upright among mankind; they all lie in wait for blood; each one hunts his brother with a net.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hasid" (H2623) denotes the one characterized by hesed — covenantal loyalty and faithfulness — here declared extinct. The hunting imagery with a "net" (herem, H2764) pictures predatory social relations, the strong ensnaring the weak. THEOLOGICAL: The complete moral collapse described here recalls the conditions of Noah's day (Gen 6:5) and anticipates eschatological lawlessness (2 Tim 3:2-4).

words.m: conjunction + particle + verb qal participle active
conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Their hands are upon evil — to do it thoroughly — the prince demands, and the judge asks for a bribe, and the great man speaks the evil desire of his soul; thus they weave it together.
The best of them is like a brier; the most upright is sharper than a thorn hedge. The day of your watchmen, your visitation, has come; now their confusion shall be.
Put no trust in a neighbor; put no confidence in a friend; guard the doors of your mouth from her who lies in your embrace.
For a son dishonors a father, a daughter rises up against her mother, a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law; a man's enemies are the men of his own house.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus quotes this verse nearly verbatim in Matthew 10:35-36 and Luke 12:53, applying it to the divisions His own coming would provoke within households. The citation indicates Micah's words had an eschatological horizon: the upheaval of the end-time visitation begins in the ministry of the Messiah. THEOLOGICAL: Family loyalty, the foundation of ancient Israelite social order, collapses under comprehensive covenant infidelity.

words.g: to/for gods in the ordinary sense

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: But as for me, I will watch expectantly for YHWH; I will wait for the God of my salvation; my God will hear me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "I will watch expectantly" (tsaphah, H6822) is a sentinel term — the prophet takes up his post as a watchman waiting for divine response. "The God of my salvation" (yeshu'ati, from yeshua, H3444) anticipates the very name of Jesus (Yeshua). THEOLOGICAL: Verse 7 is the pivotal turn of the chapter from communal lament to individual trust, structurally identical to the "turn" in many lament psalms (cf. Ps 13:5-6).

verses.flow: Rejoice not over me, O my enemy; though I fall, I shall rise; though I sit in darkness, YHWH is a light to me.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing
verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The wrath of YHWH I will bear — because I have sinned against Him — until He pleads my cause and executes judgment for me. He will bring me out to the light; I shall behold His righteousness (tsedaqah).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The speaker's willing acceptance of divine discipline ("the wrath of YHWH I will bear — because I have sinned") models genuine penitential theology. "Tsedaqah" (H6666, righteousness) here denotes YHWH's covenant-faithful vindication of the one who waits. TYPOLOGY: The movement from bearing wrath, to being brought into light, to beholding righteousness, prefigures the substitutionary and justifying work of the Messiah on behalf of sinners (Isa 53; Rom 3:26).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing
conjunction + verb piel imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: Then my enemy shall see, and shame shall cover her who said to me, "Where is YHWH your God?" My eyes shall look upon her; now she will become a trampling, like the mire of the streets.
A day for building your walls — in that day the decree shall be far removed.

words.g: and + in/with a city in the widest sense
and a sea or large body of water
from a sea or large body of water
and a mountain or range of hills
the a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: In that day they shall come to you, from Assyria and the cities of Egypt, and from Egypt to the River, from sea to sea and from mountain to mountain.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: But the earth shall become a desolation because of those who dwell in it, as the fruit of their deeds.

words.g: a collective name for a flock

verses.flow: Shepherd Your people with Your staff (shebet), the flock of Your inheritance, who dwell alone in a forest in the midst of a garden land; let them graze in Bashan and Gilead as in the days of old.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shebet" (H7626) is both "staff" and "tribe," evoking the shepherd-king imagery central to Davidic messianism. "Who dwell alone" (badad, H910) alludes to Balaam's oracle (Num 23:9), Israel's peculiar separated status among the nations. THEOLOGICAL: The prayer for Bashan and Gilead — territories lost to Assyrian conquest — expresses hope for full territorial restoration under the coming Shepherd-King (Ezek 34:23-24; John 10:11).

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: As in the days when you came out from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders (niphlaoth).
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The Exodus functions as the paradigmatic act of divine redemption throughout the prophets; here a new, greater Exodus is anticipated. "Niphlaoth" (H6381, wonders, marvels) is the vocabulary of the Exodus plagues and the Red Sea crossing (Exod 15:11). The NT presents the ministry, death, and resurrection of Yeshua as precisely this new Exodus (Luke 9:31; 1 Cor 10:1-4).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: The nations shall see and be ashamed of all their might; they shall lay their hands on their mouths; their ears shall be deaf.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: They shall lick the dust like a serpent; like the crawling things of the earth they shall come trembling out of their strongholds; they shall turn in dread to YHWH our God, and they shall be in fear of You.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The prostration of the nations before YHWH — licking dust like serpents, trembling from strongholds — describes the final subjugation of the powers that had lorded over the nations (cf. Deut 32:8; Ps 82:8). The serpent imagery (nachash) may deliberately evoke the primal enemy of Genesis 3:14, whose defeat is here extended cosmically. THEOLOGICAL: "They shall turn in dread to YHWH our God" implies a coerced rather than voluntary submission, distinguishing it from the willing worship of the redeemed remnant.

verses.flow: Who is a God like You, pardoning iniquity and passing over transgression for the remnant of His inheritance? He does not retain His anger forever, because He delights in steadfast love (hesed).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Who is a God like You?" (Mi-El kamocha) is a direct wordplay on the prophet's own name — Micah (Mikayahu) means "Who is like YHWH?" The entire book thus culminates in the answer to its own author's name. "Pardoning iniquity" (nose' avon, H5375/H5771) literally means "lifting/carrying iniquity," a term used of the scapegoat (Lev 16:22) and the Suffering Servant (Isa 53:12). TYPOLOGY: This verse is the theological summit of the book and a direct anticipation of the atoning work of Messiah Yeshua.

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing

words.g: a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: He will again have compassion (racham) upon us; He will tread our iniquities underfoot; and You will cast all their sins into the depths of the sea.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Cast all their sins into the depths of the sea" (metsuloth yam) is among the most vivid images of total divine forgiveness in all of Scripture — sins not merely covered but removed to an unretrievable depth. TYPOLOGY: The casting into the sea recalls the destruction of Egypt's army at the Red Sea (Exod 15:5, 10), making forgiveness a new Exodus event. This verse grounds the NT's proclamation of total forgiveness in Messiah (Col 2:13-14; Heb 10:17).

verses.flow: You will give faithfulness (emet) to Jacob, steadfast love (hesed) to Abraham, as You have sworn to our fathers from the days of old.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes by anchoring divine mercy in the sworn covenants to Abraham and the patriarchs — "emet" (H571, truth/faithfulness) to Jacob and "hesed" (H2617, steadfast covenantal love) to Abraham. This covenantal foundation is precisely what Paul cites in Romans 15:8-9 to explain the inclusion of both Jews and Gentiles in Messiah. TYPOLOGY: The Abrahamic covenant, never annulled, reaches its fulfillment in Yeshua, the seed of Abraham, through whom all nations receive blessing (Gal 3:16, 29).
